scenarios.opening_query: Our 'licensed and bonded' painter turned out to be neither and his prep work poisoned our yard.
scenarios.hidden_fact: He dry-scraped lead paint across the whole exterior — abatement is bid at $46,000, the state lead program is involved, and unlicensed-contractor penalties stack on top.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He dry-scraped lead paint everywhere — $46,000 abatement, the state lead program's involved, and unlicensed-contractor penalties stack.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The 'poisoning' was overspray on the roses — maybe $600, and small claims suits me fine.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It was overspray on the roses — about $600. Small claims is fine.

scenarios.opening_query: The paver company charged our card twice for the patio and calls the duplicate a 'deposit for phase two.'
scenarios.hidden_fact: There is no phase two and it's $2,200 — I want small-claims coaching and to dispute the card charge properly.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: There's no phase two — it's $2,200, and I want small-claims coaching plus the card dispute done right.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The 'phase two' they invented is a $28,000 retaining wall we never ordered — and they've recorded a pre-lien notice for it.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Their invented 'phase two' is a $28,000 wall we never ordered — with a pre-lien notice recorded.

scenarios.opening_query: Our kitchen contractor's 'final invoice' includes charges for another customer's job, and he won't correct it.
scenarios.hidden_fact: He's holding our signed permits and refuses to schedule the final inspection until we pay the padded $17,000 — the occupancy sign-off hostage is the real problem.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He's holding our permits hostage — no final inspection until we pay the padded $17,000. Occupancy is the real problem.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether permit/inspection leverage requires contractor-law remedies
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Inspections are done and passed — it's a $1,900 billing dispute I can bring myself.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Inspections passed — it's a $1,900 billing dispute I can bring myself.

scenarios.opening_query: The insulation crew left gaps everywhere and our first winter's heating bills doubled.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > Small Claims Advice || Consumer Law > General || Real Property > Construction/Contractors
scenarios.hidden_fact: They sold it door-to-door with 'guaranteed 40% savings' in writing and financed it through their own paper — the deceptive-guarantee and financing package is a consumer case bigger than the redo.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: They sold it door-to-door with a written 'guaranteed 40% savings' and their own financing — the deceptive package is bigger than the redo.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether written guarantees and seller financing create a consumer-practices case
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No guarantees or financing — a $1,700 redo of the gaps is all I'm after.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No guarantees or financing — I just want the $1,700 redo.

scenarios.opening_query: Our foundation repair company wants final payment while the crack they 'fixed' is visibly widening.
scenarios.hidden_fact: Their own warranty engineer's report admits the piering was underdesigned — $52,000 to redo per two bids, and their bond and the CCB process are in play.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Their own engineer admits the piering was underdesigned — $52,000 to redo, with their bond and the CCB in play.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The structural work held — the widening crack is cosmetic parging, a $900 fix I'll chase myself.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The structure held — it's cosmetic parging, $900, and I'll chase it myself.

scenarios.opening_query: A chimney sweep franchise 'found' $12,000 of urgent dangers that two other sweeps say don't exist.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Consumer Law > General || Wills & Trusts > Elder Abuse
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm asking for my 84-year-old mother — they've already run her card for $4,300 in 'emergency stabilization,' visit only when she's alone, and had her sign a 'maintenance plan' she can't explain; targeting her is the case.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's my 84-year-old mother — $4,300 already charged for 'emergency work,' visits timed for when she's alone, and a signed 'plan' she can't explain.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the scheme targets an elder's vulnerability
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's my own house and I'm 40 — a straightforward scare-tactic fraud claim with their written 'findings' as the evidence.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my house and I'm 40 — straight scare-tactic fraud, and I have their written 'findings.'
scenarios.vetting_note: Elder-targeting facts elevate the consumer scam.
